---
Research Title: "Conflict of AI Governance"
title: "AI Governance in Warfare"
author: "Wilson Wong"
course: "Governance of AI in Conflict"
professor: "Dr. Julian Campisi, Dr. Titilayo Soremi"
course_code: "POLD02"
Course_Name: "Independent Senior Research" 
date: "April 6th, 2026"
bibliography: ../references/references.bib
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: left
    number-sections: true
  pdf:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    documentclass: article
    geometry:
      - margin=1in
    fontsize: 12pt
    linestretch: 2
---
 

# Abstract
Artificial Intelligence (AI) has rapidly integrated into our world of conflict by reshaping how warfare works, targeting systems, and the deployment of military technology. The 2022 Russia-Ukraine war highlighted significant challenges regarding both weapons systems and the legal and governance frameworks governing AI use, raising concerns about accountability, the rules of war, and the adequacy of current international regulatory frameworks. Despite the growing usage of AI in military contexts, there remains limited empirical analysis of whether international institutions have meaningfully adapted their governance responses considering this conflict. This study will look towards the Ukraine-Russia war as a critical juncture in the global governance of military AI. Drawing on theories such as global governance and historical institutionalism, this research analyzes how international institutions respond to major security shocks and technological disruptions, especially regarding the distinction between hard-law and soft-law regulatory approaches. The study will use a qualitative research design based on document and thematic analysis of official government documents, reports, and statements issued by international institutions between 2015-2025. It will test the hypothesis that intergovernmental organizations produce a greater number of AI governance outputs in the two years after the invasion started. By examining the content of these outputs, this research assesses whether institutional responses represent incremental adaptation or more substantial governance change. The findings aim to contribute to broader debates on AI governance, global security, and institutional change in the context of modern warfare.

# Introduction

Artifical Intelligence (AI) has become one of the newly fast technologies advancement of the 21st century. It has reshape industries, governance system and most significantly the norms of armed conflict. Autonomous targeting systems, battlefieled intelligence patforms, drones, cyber operations has one element is has improve significant beucase of AI have icnreasingly become standard features of modern warfare. 

The ongoing conlifct Russia- Ukraine war with the Russia invasion of February 24, 2022 has placed these developments at the forefront of international political debate. The conflict has been described as the first large-scale conflict which AI-enabled technologies play a very key role in this conflict in the military operations, rasing urgent questions about accountability, protection of civilians, and the adequcy of current International regulatory framework (@RakhmetovMurzagulova2025;@kostenko2022).

However, as the war progress the global governanace of military AI remians fragemnted and largely non-binding. The governanace gap poses serious risks to internationa peace and security (@tallberg2023;@sweijs2024). 

This study ask a direct empircal question: Has Russia- Ukraine war constituted a critical juncture in the global governanace of Military AI? Drawing on the Historical Institutionalism (HI) and GLobal Governanace Theory, this research examines of the connection how international institutions have responded to the invasion, test whether a measurable surge in governanace outputs occured, to analysis whether the outputs represent substantive transformative change or it is only a incremental adaptation within existing law frameworks. 

This study will focus on three sprecific contributions to the literature. First, it provides an empirical evidence through a dataset of 29 offical IGO documents spanning 2015-2025 on whether and how institutional responses to the invasion change. Second, it applies Historical Institutionalism critical juncture framewrok to the domain of military AI governanace, which remains very underexplored in the existing scholarship. Lastly, it uses 1997 Ottawa Treaty on the prohibition of landmins as a governanace benchmark, offering a historically grounded standard against which establihsment of the current responses can be assessed. 

The paper will as follows. Section 2 reviews the relevant literature on AI governanace, Historical Institutionalism and the Ottawa Treaty analogy. Section 3 outlines the methodology. Section 4 presents the empirical findings, supported by charts and figures. Section 5 provides a discussion and analysis of thoses findings. 


# 2 Literature Review

Artificial Intelligence (AI) has become one of the defining technological developments of the twenty-first century, rapidly advancing across commercial, social, and military domains. In recent years, AI has transformed military operations by enabling autonomous targeting, battlefield analytics, intelligence collection, and high-precision strikes—capabilities that significantly alter how states plan, conduct, and respond to conflict. The Russia–Ukraine war, beginning in 2022, intensified global attention on the military applications of AI, demonstrating not only the strategic advantages these systems provide but also the legal, ethical, and humanitarian challenges surrounding their use in warfare. Scholars increasingly note that the conflict has accelerated debates about whether AI technologies represent a critical point in the evolution of global military governance and safety (Hendrycks, 2025; Onderco, 2025).

International institutions—including the United Nations (UN), NATO, the European Union (EU), Council of Europe and the OECD—now face growing pressure to regulate AI in ways that balance innovation, security imperatives, and the protection of civilians. Yet existing regulatory frameworks, particularly International Humanitarian Law (IHL), have been strained by the demands and implications of emerging AI systems. This has raised important questions about whether these institutions have taken meaningful steps to modernize legal norms and governance mechanisms to keep pace with technological change (Herrera Pérez, 2024; Kostenko, 2022).

This literature review therefore examines two interconnected areas of scholarship central to the research. First, it reviews theoretical perspectives—especially Historical Institutionalism—that help explain how institutions respond to technological disruption and geopolitical shocks. Second, it analyzes empirical research on AI governance, institutional responses, and the regulatory challenges associated with AI-enabled warfare. Together, these bodies of literature provide the foundation for assessing whether international institutions have adapted their approaches to governing military AI, particularly in the wake of the Russia–Ukraine conflict and the broader debates it has catalyzed.


## 2.1 Historical Institutionalism: Theoretical Foundations

The foundation of this research is rooted in institutionalism, particularly in understanding how international institutions govern AI within conflict settings. This requires examining the broader processes through which institutions develop rules and norms to regulate state behavior. Within institutionalism, this study adopts the specific stream of Historical Institutionalism (HI), which provides a framework for understanding how governance structures emerge, evolve, and follow long-term institutional trajectories.

One of the central research questions asks whether the Ukraine–Russia war represents a critical juncture in the rapid development of military AI governance. HI identifies critical junctures as periods of heightened uncertainty during which institutional change becomes possible because major events undermine existing legal and institutional paradigms (Capoccia & Kelemen, 2007). Crises such as wars, geopolitical conflicts, or technological breakthroughs often expose weaknesses in current regulatory frameworks, weakening path-dependent constraints and creating opportunities to revise policies to align with modern norms (Mahoney & Thelen, 2010).

The directly support of this research design can be offering an understanding of whether international organizations such as UN, NATO and the EU have develop a well support of AI governance structure to response to the Russia-Ukraine war. Historical Institutionalism assist between incremental institutional evolution and greater significant transformative changes that can indidcate of a true critical juncture. This distinction supports of the hypothese outlined int eh research proposal and gudies the assessment of how the policy shifts reflects continuity of meaningful institutional adaptiation.

Building from Capoccia and Kelemen, Soifer (2012) refines the logic of critical juncture by separating into two sections which is between permissive conditions: Which open a window for change and productive conditions: Which determine what kind of change actually occurs. Applied to this study, the Russia- Ukraine 2022 war constitutes the permissive condition, loosening path-dependent constraints on IGO governance. Whether productive conditions exist such as political will for binding law can determines whether the juncture produces transformative or merely incremental changes (Soifer, 2012).

The distinction is important because how path-dependent institutions tend to resist the transformation even under crisis pressure, adapting their existing framework rather than creating new binding mechanisms (Mahoney & Thelen,2010). This theoretical perspective directly supports the research design by offering a lens through which to analyze whether international organizations such as NATO, the UN, and the EU have modified their AI governance structures in response to the Ukraine–Russia war. HI also helps distinguish between incremental institutional evolution and the more significant, transformative change that would indicate the presence of a true critical juncture. This analytical distinction aligns with the hypotheses outlined in the research proposal and guides the assessment of whether observed policy shifts reflect continuity or meaningful institutional adaptation.

## 2.1 Ottawa Treaty

This historical treaty connects how global governance systems respond to threats like the Ottawa Treaty, which prohibited anti-personnel landmines. This treaty emerged from humanitarian crisis and advocacy that assist in the exposed the lack of regulatory frameworks. 

Historical institutionalist perspective, it is due to a crisis and humanitarian concerns that lead to institutional innovation. The collective agreement between states and international institutions to construct new governances’ principles when existing norms fail to address technological or military. This treaty is valuable on understanding surrounding military AI- particularly following the Russia-Ukraine war reflect similar dynamics of crisis-driven institutional adaption or instead represent of a change within existing governance structures. 

A central analytical distinction in this research of understanding is between hard and soft laws regulatory approaches. Hard-law refers to the binding instruments that impose and enforceable obligations on states (@sweijs2024). Soft-law a non-binding guidelines, principles and resolutions that carry normative weight without any legal precedents. In the context of Military AI governance, the distinction matters because of the binding frameworks require political consensus that voluntary follow and the founding of this study is mainly soft-law responses indicates institutional responsiveness without institutional transformation.

## 2.2 AI Governance in Global Affairs
The global governance of AI remains highly fragmented, and scholars widely agree that existing institutional frameworks lack the coherence necessary to regulate rapidly advancing technologies. Tallberg et al. (2023) emphasize that no single international regulatory framework governs AI, resulting in a patchwork of voluntary guidelines and ethical principles rather than binding agreements. This fragmentation is particularly problematic in the military domain, where the stakes are higher and the consequences of system failures can be severe.
International institutions have taken early steps toward addressing AI governance, but progress remains uneven across organizations. The European Union and NATO have developed structured policy approaches, issuing strategic guidelines and ethical principles for responsible AI usage in defense contexts (Osimen et al., 2024). The OECD has similarly articulated broad principles emphasizing transparency, accountability, and safety; however, these remain nonbinding and are not specifically tailored to the complexities of AI in warfare. In contrast, UN-led discussions on lethal autonomous weapons systems have faced persistent political deadlock, with member states divided over whether such weapons should be regulated, restricted, or banned outright (@herreraPerez2024).
Scholars further highlight that technological advancements often outpace institutional responses, underscoring the challenges global governance bodies encounter when attempting to regulate military AI. Hendrycks (2025) argues that the speed of AI development raises critical safety, ethical, and accountability concerns that existing governance systems are not adequately equipped to address. Sweijs and Romansky (2024) similarly contend that the time lag between technological innovation and institutional adaptation exacerbates gaps in global security norms.
Empirical literature also demonstrates that the absence of unified governance creates vulnerabilities for both civilian protection and geopolitical stability. AI-driven targeting systems, algorithmic decision-making tools, and autonomous surveillance raise substantial concerns about transparency, accountability, and humanitarian risk—issues that current international regulatory structures do not fully resolve (Shay, 2024; Roe, 2025). As a result, scholars increasingly call for more coordinated and comprehensive global governance mechanisms capable of managing the risks associated with military AI while still enabling technological innovation and operational effectiveness.


## 2.5 Institutional Responses: Strengths and Limitations
Empirical research reveals significant variation in how international institutions approach the governance of military AI. Organizations such as NATO, the UN, and the EU have established policy documents, guidelines, and regulatory frameworks intended to guide the responsible development and use of AI in defense contexts (@osimen2024. However, the extent of institutional responsiveness differs considerably across these organizations. At the UN level, progress has been particularly slow, largely due to political disagreements among member states that hinder the development of binding regulations or unified policy positions.
Despite ongoing efforts to advance standardized approaches to AI governance, no comprehensive laws or binding policies currently exist at either the national or international level. Institutional responses remain largely incremental rather than transformative, reflecting the political divisions and structural constraints that limit the ability of intergovernmental organizations (IGOs) to regulate AI effectively. This slow of incremental patterns reflects path-dependent institutional behaviour organizations adapt within existing frameworks rather than creating fundamentally new ones, even when crisis pressure exists (@mahoney2010). 
This pattern aligns with HI’s prediction that profound institutional change is unlikely without the presence of major disruptions or critical junctures. As a result, existing governance mechanisms continue to evolve slowly, reinforcing long-standing institutional trajectories rather than producing substantial reforms. 

## 2.6 The Study of Ukraine-Russia war as a critical Juncture

The literature examines whether the Ukraine–Russia war constitutes a critical juncture in the development of global AI governance and growing concerns surrounding the militarization of AI. Several scholars argue that the conflict has accelerated the adoption of AI-enabled military systems and exposed significant regulatory gaps, prompting some international institutions to revisit or expand their AI-related policies (@onderco2025). From this perspective, the war represents a period of heightened institutional uncertainty in which traditional governance frameworks no longer align with emerging technological and military realities.
Other scholars, however, contend that meaningful institutional reform remains limited. Despite increased attention to military AI, core regulatory structures—particularly International Humanitarian Law (IHL)—have not undergone substantive revision. UN negotiations on autonomous weapons continue to face political deadlock, and many institutional responses remain consistent with pre-2022 trajectories (@tallberg2023) This suggests that although the Ukraine war has generated pressure for governance reform, the level of disruption may not yet be sufficient to produce transformative institutional change.

Through the lens of Historical Institutionalism, the Ukraine–Russia conflict therefore serves as a valuable empirical case for assessing whether institutions adjust their AI governance frameworks in response to external shocks. Whether this conflict ultimately represents a true critical juncture will depend on whether IGOs implement lasting and substantial changes to their regulatory approaches—an unresolved issue in the literature and a central focus of this research.

## Conclusions 
The literature reviewed demonstrates significant and persistent concerns regarding the rapid militarization of AI and the limitations of current governance structures in regulating its use during armed conflict. Scholars consistently highlight legal uncertainties within International Humanitarian Law (IHL) and emphasize the uneven capacity of international institutions to develop robust frameworks that balance technological innovation, accountability, and civilian protection. At the same time, the Russia–Ukraine war has intensified debate over whether the conflict represents a turning point in global AI governance. Although some researchers argue that the war has accelerated institutional attention to military AI, evidence of substantive regulatory change remains mixed.

Historical Institutionalism provides an essential theoretical lens for interpreting these dynamics by emphasizing the tension between continuity and change within institutional behavior. This review underscores the importance of assessing how IGOs navigate long-standing structural constraints while responding to the pressures generated by emerging technologies. Determining whether institutions simply reinforce established practices or begin shifting toward new governance pathways is central to understanding contemporary debates on AI in warfare.

Despite the growth of scholarship on AI governance and institutional responses, notable gaps remain. Few studies systematically compare how major international organizations—including NATO, the EU, and the UN—have addressed the rapid militarization of AI, and existing research does not conclusively determine whether the Ukraine–Russia conflict has generated substantive shifts in governance practices. As a result, it remains unclear whether institutional change has been transformative or merely incremental, as predicted by Historical Institutionalism. This gap highlights the need for comparative, document-based analysis that traces institutional responses over time and evaluates whether the conflict constitutes a critical juncture in the evolution of global AI governance.

Considering these gaps, the present study will contribute to ongoing academic and policy discussions by examining whether IGOs have meaningfully advanced their AI governance frameworks in the aftermath of the Ukraine–Russia war. Through systematic analysis of institutional documents, this research aims to determine the extent to which observed changes align with theoretical expectations about crisis-driven institutional adaptation. Ultimately, this study seeks to enhance understanding of how global governance structures can better address the challenges posed by rapidly advancing military technologies and ensure that AI remains subject to responsible, transparent, and accountable oversight.

# 3 Methodology Section

## 3.1 Research Section
This study will conduct a qualitative comparative document analysis to futher understand how international governmental organizations (IGOs) responded to the governanace of military domain in artifical intelligence (AI) in the post-invasion of the Russia- Ukraine invasions (2022). This approach is appropriate because of the research questions concerns of international behaviour, nromative change and governanace outputs which puts a phenomena of interpretive analysis rather than statistical measurement. The document analysis is suitable for this research because official institutional documents reflect the formal positions, tone and regulatory commitments of organisations at specific historical moments. This context of this study, official IGO dcouments has constitute direct primary evidence of institutional responses to challenge of military AI governanace, enabling systematic comparison of outputs before and after the critical date of February 24, 20222. 

This selection is to only focus on IGO-level documents for this empirical literature. As @tallberg2023 demonstrate is how international institutions is the primary sites in which how AI governanace norms are developed and disseminated on the international stage. @sweijs2024 further contested that IGOs represent the main arena for the development of international norms on AI military, making their offical outputs of the most appropriate unit of analysis for the study concerned with governanace- level change. 

## 3.2 Process Tracing
This study employs of process tracing as its core analytical strategy. Process tracing identifies the causal mechanism connecting an independent variable which the February 2022 invasion to a dependent variable which is the volume and binding character of IGO AI governanace outputs. it is very unlike purely descriptive approaches as process tracing allows researchers to examine whether a causal pathway links a critical juncture to institutional change (@capoccia2007). The mechanism proposed is as establish: the invasion constituted a major external shock that exposed governanaace gaps, lossenining pathdependent institutional constraints and creating a window of opporunity. @soifer2012 permissive and productive conditions framework guides the assessment of whether if its the window was used to generate trasnformative change.

## 3.3 Dataset Establish
This study conduct of 29 offical IGO documents proced betwen 2017- 2025 across five international organizations: the United Nations (UN), North Atlantic Treaty Organizatiosn (NATO), European Union (EU), rganisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) and the Council of Europe (COE). These institutions is selected because it preprent the primary multillateral governance in which how Artifical Intelligence governanace norms are being establish as they collectively account for the greater and widely cited governance outputs in the period under study (@tallberg2023;@osimen2024). 

These documents sourced from offical institutional archives, government datasets and authenticated policy databases to ensure of there authenticity and validity in the research. The inclusion criteria required each document must constitute a fromal instituional output which is including resolutions, policy briefs, adopted framework, treaty, acts and offical AI governanace principles that is issued by one of the five designated IGOs. National governmants, NGOs reports were excluded to maintain focus on formal intergovernmental governanace outputs. 

The cricial divided is February 24th 2022 as this is the date where the invasion had begin. Documents are classified as pre-invasion (N=4) or post-invasion (N=25) it is based on its publication date to divided the document. This classification allows a direct empirical comparison of these instituional output amounts and its character before and after the invasion. Which allows to built a foundation for assessing whether the conflict constitutes a critical juncture in the governanace of military AI (@capoccia2007). 

## Analytical Framework

The study will apply Historical Instituitonalism (HI) as its primary tehoretical lens. HI assist a the framework to understand how governanace strctures emerge, envolve and establish for long-term institutional trajectories shaped by a path-dependent constraints (@thelen1999). As @mahoney2010 establish, during crisis conditions, instituiotn tend lends towards incremental adaptation rather than transformative change using existing rules and norms rather adding or replacing it. This establsihment is the central to the study: the Russia- Ukraine invasion may have triggered a significant increase in IGO activites in governanace while stopping well short of biding hard-law instruments that a full critical juncture would require. 

Besides HI, Global Governanace Theory provides a complmentary framework. @tallberg2023 argues threats in a transnational context can generate pressure for a collboration IGO responses, but this responses frequently remain very fragemented and non-binding in practice. The forgotten of a unified governnace framework on goverence of military AI despite greater recongnitions of the risks without shows a broader pattern of soft-law dominance oberserved across the dataset (@herreraPerez2024; @sweijs2024). 

A central distinction in this research is hard law and soft law regulatory approachs. Hard law refers to be legally binding instruments: Treaties, conventions and acts can be enforceable. Soft law is non bidning, principles, resolutions that carry nromative influces without any legal obligations nor enforcement. @sweijs2024 establish a distinction of foundational to evaluating progress in AI governance in millitary domain, that the current stage of international landscape is heavily dominated by soft-laws. from the 29 documents in the datasets provides a empirical basis for understadning whether post-invasion governanace outputs reflects the substantive change or the continuation of the current performances and soft-law preferences. 

## Ottawa Treaty Benchmark
This research uses the Ottawa Treaty (1997) it is the Convention on the Prohibition of the Use, Stockpiling, Production and Transfer of Anti-personnel Mines and on their Destruction to be a comparative governanace benchmark. It as been selected becuase it represents a case of crisis driven into hard law success in international security governanace. it emerged from crisis to a legally binding obligation with defined law and achieved near- universal state adoption within a short period. From the perspective of Historical Institutionalism, this treaty illustrates a case which permissive conditions were accompanied by sufficient productve conditions haivng the political will to generate transformative binding law. (@capoccia2007;@mahoney2010). 

The Benchamrks will be appling not only suggest military AI governanace must follow a 100% pathway but to allow a measurable and historically ground standard aginst which current IGO responses can be assessed. However, this benchmark has fall short on governanace AI in terms of binding obligation, agreed definitions and paricipation by major AI powers. This gap establish evidnece that the critical juncture has produced institutional change but not fully transformation. This approach is consistent with existing research, which frequently reference the Ottawa Treaty as a model as a benchmark of its emerging international security governanace frameworks (@sweijs2024; @herreraPerez2024).

# Limitations
There were serveral limitations must be acknowledged. First, the Russia-Ukraine war remains ongoing, meaning some documents may be incomplete or subject to revision. As @hendrycks2025 notes, the rapid development of AI will creates challenges for governanace research, as intsituional responses may be lag behind technological relities. Second, the study only focuses on IGO-levels governanaces and does not examine States AI defence politics. Finally, the focus on five institutions, UN, NATO, EU, OECD and COE amy undeprresent governanace activities occuring in other states or bilateral forums. Despite these limitations, this study heavily reliance on official primary sources, acedmic artcles and a clearly oeperationalised theoritecial framework grounded in Historical Institutionalism and Global Governance Theory provides a rigorous and reproducible basis for its findings. 

# 4 Findings 

This presents the empirical findings drawn from the systematic analysis of 29 offical intergovernmental Organizations (IGO) documents produced between 2018 and 2025 with the selected five international institutions: the United Nations (UN), The North Atlantic Treaty Organizations (NATO), The European Union (EU), The Organisation for Economic Co-operation Development (OECD) and the Council of Europe (COE). The findings address the research questions of whether the Russia- Ukraine war constitutes a critical juncture in the global governanace of military Artificial Intelligence (AI). 

## Finding One: Increased IGO Activity

The significant and direct finding is the sharp increase in IGO governanace outputs after february 24, 2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine.before the invasions the dataset only have four documents produced across the period of 2018-2021, with no documents has been publish before 2017. After the invasion has starts from 2022 to 2025, the dataset contains more 25 additional documents leading a 500% increases in governanace actions. This increase is not evenly spread in the post-invasion period. 2024 is surges occurs which alone accoutns 12 of the 29 documents in the full dataset, follwed by 2025 which accounts for a further nine documents. The years 2022 and 2023 produced modest outpus as it is suggesting the gvoernanace response built progessively rather than emerging immediately. 

The pattern establish is consisent with @capoccia2007 which describes the elements of cirtical juncture: a period of institutional disruption may not necessarily able to estalish immediate governanace change, but rather open for opporunities on the institional activity follows. The icnrease of trajectory after the invasions provides an strong empirical understanding for the hypothesis that Russia-Ukraine provide a major priority shifts in IGO governanace hehaviour. 

  Figure 1

# 4.2 Soft-Law Dominance

The establishment of the war had lead to the increase in governanace volume, the content of those outputs has shown a fundamental limtation: 83% of the 29 documents are non-binding instruments. As there is only firve document constitute a binding and enforceable: the EU AI Act (2024), the Council of Europe Framework Convention on AI and HUman Rights (2024) and the three OECD Revised Recommendations. The remaining 24 documents are consider non-binding guidelines, principles and white papers. 

The establishment of this pattern is consistent by @long2025 which reinforced of how institutions responded by producing more to justify what they arleady knew. It rather than negoitating binding treaty commitments. @sweijs2024 identify this as systemic in the military AI governanace space: structural disincentives for major power agreement making soft-law the realistic ceiliing of IGO responses. Figure 3 has shown. 

Figure 3 


## 4.3 UN Dominance 

Majority of finding doucments is from the United Nations (UN) making the key actor by a sustaintial margin. the UN dominanace is significant given that the general Assembly which is the responsible for most of these outputs operates under majority vote and produces resolutions that carry no legal obligation. The UN's 18 documents include 14 post-2022 resolutions or reports, yet none constitute binding law. 

As it reflect on @tallberg2023 describe as a strctural feature of global AI Governaance: the UN system has only in the broadest terms to voice concerns but yet the limiations its the capacity to produce bidning instruments, as consensus among deeply divded major powers is establishing. The UN Secondary- General's 2025 report on AI in the military domain explicitly call for binding governanace but produced no binding governace law showing the gap between declaratory ambition and institutional output (@onderco2025;@osimen2024). 

## 4.4 Ottawa Treaty Benchmark Not Met

The Benchmark from the Ottawa Treaty does not match with the military AI goveranance which falls short on the 3 elements of hard-law governanace success. First, there is no bidning military AI treaty in force. second there is no major Ai powers states as it remains outside any binding military AI Framewrok. Lastly, there is no fully agreed definition of military AI system or Lethal Autonomous Weapons Systems (LAWS). As seen in the effort compare to the Ottawa treaty which has a comparable understanding and definitions that is reconize to enforce and obligated to follow by the states. 

Figure 4: Ottawa

The Finding of the core empirical limitation of critical juncture identified in Finding One. While the invasion constituted a permissive condition that opened a chance of governanace window (@soifer2012), the productive conditions for a transformative bidning enforcement did not materialise in the post-invasion period (@herreraPerez2024; @sweijs2024). 

# 5 Discussion and Anlysis

The finding supports of the central argument of this study of how the Russia- Ukraine war triggered a heighten level of policymaking activites directed of the use of AI technology in warfare. However eventhough there is increase of outputs it did not trigger transformative change. This section interprets each finding through the theoretical framewroks establish in the literature review. 

## 5.1 Partial Critical Juncture 

The surge of 525% in governanace outputs between the pre and post invasion period which contitutes a strong empirical evidence on Russia-Ukraine war functions as a permissive condition in (@soifer2012) framework.The discontinuity between the four pre-invasion document and the post invasion document is precisely the pattern thet @capoccia2007 assoicate with critical juncturers in historical institutionalism (HI): a sudden break from prior institutional trajectory. however, the cirtical juncturer is partial rather than full. A full critical juncture would define as would require the decisions made during this window to produce a fundamentally new path from governanace party. Which in this case a binding multilateral treay on military AI regulations which this did not occur. 

The post-invasion pattern instead consistent with @long2025 reinfroced similar pathway: institutions when produce more governanace activity in any kind without crossing the threshold into binding law. Therefore, a critical juncture in gvoernanace colume but not in the depth of governanace treaty. This distinction carries greater implication for how scholars and policymakers interpret the apparent increase in institutional activity which the golime of outputes does not show a sufficient measure of goveranance effectiveness. 

## 5.2 The Soft-Law Trap

The soft-law dominance raises the fundamental question: is soft-law a stepping stone towards harder regulations or is it just a substitue for it? @sweijs2024 points the latter is the more likely outcome in the current geoplitical environemnt. At the beginning it may be seen as a sigal of normative commitment and respond to public pressure without requiring the diffcult a political negotiations that binding treates requires. As the perspective from institutions operating in a context of deep major power fragment and rivalry, soft-law approach may be the only politically achievable output. 

This analysis does not dismiss the sprit and value of soft-law governanace. The OECD's Revised Recommendations (2019, 2024), NATO AI Principles (2021,2024) and the UN resolution on LAWS all together establish a normative framework on the states behaviour even without binding force. As @mahoney2010 note, incremental institutional change through layering still able to show cumulative effort over time. However,  the Ottawa Treaty benchmark it against of the soft-law regime as it does not provides no verification mechanism, no obligations nor enforcement capacity compare to Ottawa treaty. The gap between the normative declaration of IGOs and the operational relity of AI deployment in under military domain as documented by @RakhmetovMurzagulova2025 and @techreport{geneva_academy_ai_military_2025} shows of the potential increase of risk and harm without a binding governanace framework.

# 5.3 Asymmetric Institutional Responses 

The United Nations produced over 50% of all goveranance documents as majority is non-binding resolutions which signle of a structural asymmetry of the current governanace landscape. Other institutions with the broadest membership and legitimacy can be precisely those least capable of producing binding elements. The instituitons have produce hard-law like the EU and COE can operate within a much smaller membership within the Western-aligned states. What this establish of the most significant hard-law governanace innovations in this area apply to a subset of internatioanl communities rather to a global system. 

As @tallberg2023 of his analysis in fragmented AI governanace and it is undermines the Ottawa Treaty: the Mine Ban Treaty succeeded precisely becuase of a major crisis of how the weapon joined the regime. A military AI governanace framework in this case if there is not any greater transfomrative action from this increment action today there is no point or more action can be consider as @onderco2025 reaches similar ending arguing that responsible use declarations without binding obligations cannot consitiute regulation in any meaningful transformative sense. @ojha2024 similar argues of how the strucutral gaps in International Humanitarian Law (IHL) which is the only primary law governing in armed conflict has not designed for autonomous systems and AI at all which it needs for greater corrdinated multilateral action rather than unilateral institutional declarations. 

# 5.4 Theoretical Confirmation and Qualification

Historical Institutionalism's (HI) in this study substantially supports in this study. The framework predicted there would be a major external shock would increase institutional activity which is confirmed by the 525% surge. It also confirm of institutions pathways would go towards incremental adaptation instead of transformative change (@mahoney2010) showing by the soft-law principle finding. @long2025 typology of reinforced provides the fits for the observed pattern of governance change. Global Governance Theory prediction  of that transnational threats would built up as more fragmented and non-binding responses (@tallberg2023) which confirmed in depth terms, as no unified binding framework emerged despite the sure in soft-law outputs.

## 5.5 Policy Implications

The finding shows the effect of impact for policymakers. The legal capacity does not truly exist in the military domain. As there is AI law by the EU AI Act and the COE Framework Convention prove that binding instruments are possible. However, there is political will from states with the first AI Act establish by the European Union (EU) under the act Artcle 2 which exempt Military AI system from the act (@eu2024aiact). This exemption is a clear sign is the political will of producing a AI law is not equipt. Future governanace progress likely will requires greater targeted advocacy and diplomatic efforts to have a treaty under condition of geoplitical rivalry. the technical argument advanace by (@simmonsEdler2025) which AI research must be directly involved in the regulatory lifecycle to point to the kind of epistemic community bulding that preceded the Ottawa Treaty process. 
# Conclusions

The study set out to answer a single question: Does the Russia- Ukraine war constitute a critical juncture in the global governanace of military AI? The answer, would be yes but only partially after anlysising 29 offical IGO documents between 2015- 2025. 

The invasions did triggerd a 525% increase in IGO governanace documents post-invasion. while it is confim that the conflict constituted a permissive condition of a path-dependent institutional constraints lossened and governanace activity has expecited especially the 5 major internatioanl institutions. The UN, NATO, EU, OECD and COE which prodced more governanace outputs in the past three years follwing of the invasion in 2022. It represents a measurable and historically significant break from the pre-invasion trajectory, consistent with the theoretical definition of a crtiical juncture by @capoccia2007. However, the post-invasion governanace increase is not a coincidence but rather a direct institutional response to the governanace gap exposed by the conflict. 

However, the prodcutive for the transformative governanace change is not sufficient. 83% of post-invasion outputs are non-binding, soft- law. which there is no agreement that can be enforceable by IGOs. The measured against the Ottawa Treaty benchmark, military AI governanace in 2025 does not meet the conditions of the binding governanace of the 1997 Mine Ban Treaty demonstrated were achievable. The juncture produce on the path of institutional reinforcement rather than institutional transformation relates to @mahoney2010 account of incremental change and @long2025 typology of reinforcement. 

The contribution of the study is threefold. Firstly it provides a systematic document base evidence of whether the Russia- Ukraine war shaped of how should the world governs AI in conflict. Theoretically, it demostrates the applicability of Historical Institutionalism's critical juncture framework to the domain of military AI governanace. With the perspective of policy there is specific condition has been identifies which is the political will among major Ai power, binding treaty negoitation would be required to close the governaance gap documented in this study. 

Future research should examine whether the 2026- 2028 period can produces hard law whether the governanace frameworks adopted by european institutions can serves as an experiences and framework on a multilarteral agreement to show a insitutional transformation. The Russia-Ukraine war showed that the question fo military AI governanace can no longer be deferred. but what is not yet shown is whether the international community has the political will to produce this framework internationally it. 

# References
